narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 47% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to raise concerns about your readiness. You have a solid profile in some areas, like ranking in the top decile for your Year 1 grades at school, an undergraduate GPA right around the typical 3.2, upper-middle family income, and being a full-time student, which is the norm for most applicants. You're also a white female, aligning with common demographics in the applicant pool. However, the model focused most heavily on your Year 3 school decile, where you ranked in the bottom group—far below the average—making it the strongest factor nudging the prediction toward potential bar failure. Next in influence was your strong Year 1 performance, which surprisingly also contributed slightly toward the failure prediction in this context, perhaps because it highlighted a later drop-off. Your family income level followed closely as another factor pushing in that direction. Then came your LSAT score of 28.5, well below the average of about 36.7, adding to the model's concerns about bar success. Your undergraduate GPA, race, gender, and full-time status had progressively smaller influences, all leaning toward the riskier outcome. Compared to typical students, your early academic strength is a bright spot, but the sharp decline by Year 3 and lower LSAT stand out as deviations that amplified the risk signal. Overall, no single factor doomed your application, but these top influences combined to tip the scales. To boost your future chances, focus on rebuilding consistent academic performance like your Year 1 success—perhaps through better study habits or tutoring to avoid late-year dips. Consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average score, as that's a key predictor you can directly improve. With targeted preparation, you could strengthen these areas and reapply stronger for the bar and admission. Keep pushing; many students turn things around with focused effort.
